note_data.title: Electrical quantities
keyPoints: **Electric current** is the rate of flow of charge and is measured in amperes (A)
**Voltage (potential difference)** is the energy transferred per unit charge and is measured in volts (V)
**Resistance** is the opposition to current flow and is measured in ohms (Ω)
In a **series circuit**, current is the same at all points but voltage is shared across components
In a **parallel circuit**, voltage is the same across all branches but current is shared between branches
**Electrical energy** is converted from chemical energy in batteries or kinetic energy in generators
**Electrical power** is the rate of energy transfer and is measured in watts (W)
**Work done** by electricity equals energy transferred and is measured in joules (J)
Current flows from positive to negative terminal in conventional current direction
Total resistance in series circuits is the sum of individual resistances

definitions.definition: The rate of flow of electric charge
The energy transferred per unit charge
The opposition to the flow of electric current
The rate of transfer of electrical energy
The energy transferred by an electric current

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and units
State similarities AND differences between the items

modelAnswer.question: A 12V battery is connected to a 4Ω resistor. Calculate (a) the current flowing through the resistor (b) the power dissipated by the resistor.
modelAnswer.answer: (a) Using V = IR, I = V/R = 12V ÷ 4Ω = 3A (b) Using P = I²R, P = (3A)² × 4Ω = 9 × 4 = 36W
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Correct formula P = I²R or P = VI

commonMistakes: Never confuse current and charge - current is rate of flow of charge, not charge itself
Students often add resistances in parallel circuits - remember to use 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂
Don't forget units in calculations - current (A), voltage (V), resistance (Ω), power (W)
Never say voltage 'flows' - voltage is a difference, current flows
Students mix up series and parallel rules for current and voltage distribution
examinerTips: This topic appears in every paper - master the calculations
Always show your working step by step for calculation questions
Draw circuit diagrams clearly with correct symbols
Remember to state the correct formula before substituting values
Power calculations often use Pakistani appliances like fans or electric heaters as context
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Circuit diagram showing battery, resistor, ammeter (in series) and voltmeter (in parallel across resistor) with correct circuit symbols and current direction marked
